text: This is an interesting laparoscopic splenectomy for ITP.
The patient is a sixty-three year old healthy male.
The surgeon begins the laparoscopic splenectomy by mobilizing the splenic flexure, with the patient positioned in the left decubitus position.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac after mobilizing the splenic flexure, positioning near the splenic hilum and anterior to the pancreas.
The laparoscopic view reveals an atrophic pancreas positioned more medially than usual, adjacent to the splenic hilum.
The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon positioned directly above the splenic hilum, with surrounding anatomical structures visible.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac and begins to focus on the splenic pedicle, preparing for further dissection around the splenic hilum.
The surgeon encounters minor bleeding in the surgical field near the splenic hilum, requiring attention to control the bleeding.
The patient's platelets are still in the fifties.
The surgeon manually controls the area to prevent further bleeding and places Surgicel over the site to manage the situation temporarily.
The surgical field is well lit, displaying a mix of red from bleeding and other preserved tissue colors around the splenic hilum.
There's good preservation of color, which is the advantage of using a good optical system such as the four K system.
The surgeon mobilizes the short gastric vessels and opens the lesser sac, improving access and visibility in the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates through the intra-abdominal fat to access the lesser sac, which is now clearly visible despite the surrounding adipose tissue.
The surgeon prepares to fire a surgical stapler across the splenic pedicle, aiming to secure the area and prevent further bleeding.
I have to be careful because this patient has a history of ITP so I cannot damage the spleen in any way.
The surgeon introduces the stapler into the surgical field, preparing to fire it across the pedicle.
The surgical stapler slides smoothly into position across the splenic pedicle, ready for firing.
But in a moment things are going to get very eventful.
So I will let the tape just keep running.
The surgeon positions the stapler carefully around the spleen, ensuring it is well placed for closure without causing damage.
The surgeon fires the stapler, resulting in massive bleeding from the surgical site.
When this happens, the key is to remain calm and to try to gain control as quickly as possible.
And that is what I was able to achieve in this particular case.
The surgeon uses a grasper to secure tissue beneath the existing grasper, aiming to gain a better hold on the area responsible for bleeding.
The surgeon examines the area, identifying either the splenic pedicle or a large short gastric pedicle or branch as the source of concern.
Now you can take the time to calm down, relax, and reevaluate using the suction and additional graspers if necessary.
The surgeon switches to an assistance grasper to gain control over the bleeding area, securing the tissue for better management.
Firing clips in at this point would be a bad idea as clips would diminish my ability to use the devices that are essential for safety, such as energy devices like the Thunder Beat in this case.
And likewise, clips can make it very difficult, if not impossible, to safely staple.
And the Thunder Beat and the staplers are going to be the key tools for safe completion of this operation.
The assistant's hand remains steady, holding the spleen in place while the surgeon carefully dissects the short gastric and retroperitoneal attachments to the spleen.
I have to again be very careful not to rip the spleen or damage it in any way.
The surgeon reintroduces the stapler, carefully passing it underneath the previously damaged area to control the vascular inflow and manage bleeding.
After closing the vessel, the surgeon observes some back bleeding, which appears manageable within the surgical field.
The surgeon fires the stapler while observing the surgical field to ensure the safe completion of the splenectomy, with visible attention to the surrounding anatomical structures.
Everything seems to be under control.
The surgeon places an additional stapler across the mobilized spleen to complete the resection, ensuring the spleen is not damaged during the process.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field, observing no active bleeding from the previously addressed vascular area.
The surgeon uses suction to remove all visible clots from the surgical field, ensuring a clear view of the area.
The surgical field appears red, but there is no visible active bleeding.
The surgeon places clips at various locations within the surgical field for safety, ensuring secure closure of vessels or tissue structures.
The surgeon places the resected spleen specimen into a retrieval bag and morselizes it through a 15mm trocar, ensuring no spillage occurs.
Post-operatively the patient did very well and has had no further issues.
